mnemonics.title: PRIMITIVE DATA TYPES MEMORY SIZE
mnemonics.content: INT: 4 BYTES, FLOAT: 4 BYTES, DOUBLE: 8 BYTES, CHAR: 1 BYTE - IF YOU CAN FIND CARELESS DATA MANAGERS

mnemonics.title: DATA STRUCTURE OPERATIONS
mnemonics.content: TIDS - TRAVERSING, INSERTION, DELETION, SEARCHING - TIDS HELP FIND DATA QUICKLY

mnemonics.title: ARRAY ADVANTAGES
mnemonics.content: FAST ACCESS - CONTIGUOUS MEMORY ALLOWS DIRECT INDEX ACCESS IN O(1)

mnemonics.title: STATIC VS DYNAMIC ALLOCATION
mnemonics.content: STATIC: SLOW BUT SAFE - DYNAMIC: FLEXIBLE BUT CAREFUL - STATIC STACK, DYNAMIC HEAP

acronyms.usage: Describes how Stack data structure operates - last element inserted is first to be removed
Describes how Queue data structure operates - first element inserted is first to be removed
Allocating memory at runtime using malloc(), calloc(), or new operators
Programming paradigm that uses objects, often utilizing data structures like classes and linked lists
Mathematical model defining data structure behavior without implementation details

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a row of numbered lockers in a dorm. Each locker has an index number and stores one item of the same type. They are adjacent to each other.
Think of a large seating chart in a stadium where most seats are empty. Instead of marking every empty seat, you only note the occupied ones with their row and seat number.
Structure is like separate rooms in a house, each with its own space. Union is like a single room that can be used as bedroom, living room, or office - but only one at a time.
Row-major: read a book page by page (across rows). Column-major: read vertically down each column first, then move to the next column.
Think of hotel rooms being assigned. Static allocation is like having fixed pre-assigned rooms. Dynamic allocation is like checking guests into available rooms as they arrive.

analogies.analogy: Like apartment building rooms - all same size, numbered sequentially, can directly go to room number 5 without checking rooms 1-4.
Like a house address written on a paper - the paper (pointer) doesn't contain the house, but tells you where to find it.
Like a contact list where most of your friends have moved away - instead of keeping track of everyone, you only keep addresses of the few who still live nearby.
Like a stretching rubber band - starts small but can grow when you need more space, unlike a rigid wooden stick.
Like a student ID card containing multiple fields: name, roll number, photo, all stored together.
Like a multi-purpose room that can be rearranged - can be a meeting room OR a dining room OR a dance floor, but only one use at a time.
